codeReview.summary: All three components (ThreatClock, RiskGauge, BranchMap) are well-implemented as reusable, purely presentational SVG React components with typed readonly props, CSS transitions, and comprehensive test coverage (26 tests). The implementation correctly replaces inline SweepClock/BranchBar in App.tsx with the new reusable components via adapter functions. Accessibility attributes (role=img, aria-label) are present on all components. Minor non-blocking issues noted below.

issues.description: valueToDeg uses Math.min(value, max) but does not guard against negative values. If value < 0, the hand will render beyond the start position (-135°). Should add Math.max(value, 0) for defensive clamping.

gradId is derived from label via string replacement. If two RiskGauge instances share the same label prop, their linearGradient ids will collide, causing one gauge to use the other's gradient. Consider adding a unique suffix (e.g., React.useId() or a counter).

Component defines its own ThreatClockSignal/ThreatClockProps interfaces instead of importing from @workspace/radar-core. Feature description says 'accept typed props (from radar-core types)'. While pragmatic (radar-core types are Zod schema-based, not ideal for component props), the local types could drift from the canonical schema over time.

Inconsistent wrapper pattern: ThreatClock returns bare SVG, RiskGauge wraps in div+span, BranchMap wraps in div+SVG. This inconsistency could cause unexpected layout behavior when composing components together.

No responsive breakpoints added for the new .signal-gauges grid or component sizing. Existing media queries at 480px only cover old layout elements. On small screens, the gauge grid and BranchMap may overflow.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker skipped agent-browser visual verification (step 5 of frontend-worker skill procedure) but reported followedProcedure: true in skillFeedback. The skill explicitly says 'Verify visually with agent-browser' including opening the dev server, taking screenshots, and checking animations. Worker only ran typecheck, tests, and build — no visual validation was performed.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Transcript skeleton shows zero agent-browser tool calls. Handoff verification.commandsRun contains only tsc, vitest, pnpm build, and vitest -t. No interactiveChecks array present in handoff. skillFeedback.followedProcedure = true with empty deviations array.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker installed vitest 1.6.x in threat-radar-web, but architecture.md documents 'Workspace uses vitest 0.34.6'. This creates version fragmentation — the subproject now uses a different vitest major version than the workspace baseline. The features.json verificationSteps use --grep which doesn't work in vitest 1.x (uses -t instead).
sharedStateObservations.evidence: package.json diff shows vitest ^1.6.1 added. architecture.md Testing Notes section says 'vitest 0.34.6 which does NOT support --grep flag'. Worker's discoveredIssues notes the --grep vs -t discrepancy.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Components define local TypeScript interfaces for props rather than importing/extending types from @workspace/radar-core. AGENTS.md coding conventions say 'Use @workspace/* scope for shared packages' but don't explicitly mandate importing types for component props. The feature description says 'from radar-core types'. This creates a type duplication risk where component props could diverge from the canonical radar-core schema.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: ThreatClock.tsx defines ThreatClockSignal and ThreatClockProps locally (lines 8-28). BranchMap.tsx defines BranchMapBranch locally (lines 6-19). radar-core schema.ts exports ReducedSnapshot, reducedSignalSchema, reducedBranchSchema which contain the canonical field shapes.

sharedStateObservations.observation: Worker had to trial-and-error vitest version compatibility — first installed vitest 4.1.0 (needs vite ^6), then downgraded to ^1.6.0. architecture.md documents vitest 0.34.6 but doesn't mention vite version constraints or recommended vitest version for subprojects using vite 5.x. Adding a note about compatible vitest versions for vite 5 projects would save future workers this iteration.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Transcript shows: 'I see vitest 4.1.0 was installed but it needs vite ^6. Let me downgrade to a compatible version' followed by pnpm add -D vitest@^1.6.0.

summary: PASS. All three SVG React components (ThreatClock, RiskGauge, BranchMap) are implemented correctly as reusable presentational components with typed props, CSS transitions, and 26 passing vitest tests. Components are properly integrated into App.tsx replacing inline implementations. The commit includes vitest configuration, test setup, barrel exports, and CSS styles. Five non-blocking issues noted: missing negative value guard in ThreatClock, potential gradient ID collision in RiskGauge, local type definitions rather than radar-core imports, inconsistent component wrapper patterns, and missing responsive breakpoints. Key shared-state observation: worker skipped agent-browser visual verification despite skill procedure requiring it.
